import * as React from 'react'; import { Link, useParams } from 'react-router-dom'; import { Clock, BookOpen, Users, Star, Play, Award, Target, User, Building, FileText, Calendar, Shield, Scale, Eye, Lock, AlertTriangle, Key, Camera } from 'lucide-react'; const ScenarioDetail: React.FC = () => { const { id } = useParams<{ id: string }>(); // Comprehensive scenario data based on the user's provided narrative const scenario = { _id: '1', title: 'The Inventory That Changed Everything', subtitle: 'First-Time Bail Application – Theft Offense', description: 'A sales executive is accused of theft after a valuable laptop goes missing during an inventory check. As his defense attorney, you must investigate the case, interview the client, research relevant laws, and argue for bail. The truth is hidden in the details - can you uncover what really happened?', caseOverview: `“The Inventory That Changed Everything” A Complete Narrative of the Rajesh Kumar Theft Case Chapter 1: A Routine Morning October 15, 2025, began like any other for Rajesh Kumar. He woke at 6:00 a.m. in his small but tidy apartment in Basavanagudi, Bangalore. His two-year-old daughter, Ananya, was already babbling in her crib. His wife, Priya, handed him a steaming cup of tea and reminded him: "Don't forget—inventory day today." Rajesh smiled. "I've done it a dozen times. Nothing to worry about." For four years, Rajesh had worked as a sales executive at Vijay Electronics, a well-known local chain that sold everything from smartphones to home appliances. He wasn't management, but he was trusted—punctual, honest, and always willing to help during audits. His boss often said, "If Rajesh says it's there, it's there." That morning, he wore his usual attire: pressed trousers, a light blue shirt, and his company ID clipped to his pocket. Slung over his shoulder was a dark navy fabric bag—a gift from Priya on their wedding anniversary. Inside: his lunchbox, a water bottle, and a slim black mobile charger in a hard plastic case. Chapter 2: The Inventory Room At 10:45 a.m., Rajesh reported to the first-floor storage room. The monthly inventory had begun. This month, the focus was on high-value IT assets, including five new laptops purchased for the marketing team. One of them—a Dell Inspiron valued at ₹45,000—had just been unboxed the day before. Rajesh was paired with Prakash Mehta, a colleague of six years. They worked in comfortable silence, scanning barcodes and ticking off items on a printed sheet. At 11:02 a.m., Rajesh bent down to pick up his charger, which had slipped from his bag when he set it on the floor. "Careful," Prakash joked. "Don't lose your lifeline!" Rajesh chuckled and dropped the charger back into his bag. The entire motion took less than three seconds. Unbeknownst to them, a ceiling-mounted CCTV camera in the hallway—meant to monitor access to the storage room—captured the moment. Due to its high angle and low resolution, the charger's hard case appeared rectangular and substantial, almost like a slim laptop. Chapter 3: The Disappearance By 2:00 p.m., the inventory team hit a snag. Four laptops were accounted for. The fifth—the ₹45,000 Dell—was nowhere to be found. Panic rippled through the office. The store manager, Mr. Desai, immediately locked the storage room and pulled up the CCTV footage. He watched as Rajesh entered at 11:03 a.m., stayed for 14 minutes, and exited—placing something into his bag. "No one else went in," Desai muttered. "It has to be him." When confronted, Rajesh turned pale. "That wasn't the laptop! It was my charger—I swear!" Prakash backed him up: "He's telling the truth. I was right there." But Desai was under pressure. The laptop hadn't been logged out to anyone. And the footage... it looked damning. Chapter 4: The Twist – A Hidden Detail Later that evening, while reviewing the digital inventory log, a junior clerk noticed something odd: The missing laptop had been marked "dispatched to Service Center" at 9:47 a.m.—before the inventory even began. But there was no service ticket, no technician signature, and no record at the service center. Someone had falsified the digital log. Was Rajesh being framed? Or was this a red herring? The police, however, weren't swayed. "The CCTV shows him taking something," the investigating officer said. "Until we find that laptop—or prove it was never there—your client is our prime suspect." Chapter 5: Arrest and Aftermath At 7:30 a.m. on October 16, two officers knocked on Rajesh's door. Priya burst into tears. Ananya clung to her father's leg. Rajesh didn't resist. "I'll go peacefully. But I didn't do this." At the police station, he was calm but shaken. When asked if he had a passport, he pulled it from his wallet without hesitation. "Take it. I'm not running. I have a daughter who needs me." He named his father, Ramesh Kumar—a respected grocer in Shankarapuram—and his brother-in-law, Arjun Rao, a school teacher, as sureties. Both arrived within the hour, offering whatever it took to bring Rajesh home. But the law was clear: theft of property valued over ₹5,000 is non-bailable under Indian law. Rajesh would have to apply for bail in court. Chapter 6: The Unanswered Questions As of October 17, the morning of the bail hearing, several facts remained unresolved: • The laptop had not been recovered—not in Rajesh's home, not in his bag, not anywhere. • Prakash stood by Rajesh, but admitted he never saw what was placed in the bag. • The falsified inventory log suggested possible internal fraud—but no other employee had been investigated. • Rajesh had no motive: he earned ₹42,000/month, had no debts, and had recently been considered for a promotion. • His passport surrender, family ties, job stability, and clean record all pointed to low flight risk. Yet the prosecution would argue: "An employee stole a high-value asset in a position of trust. The evidence is on camera. Bail would undermine the investigation." Rajesh sat in his cell that night, staring at a photo of Ananya on his phone (confiscated but briefly returned for a call). He whispered, "I just want to be there for her birthday next week." Chapter 7: On the Eve of Justice The story does not end with a verdict—because this is where the legal process begins. Rajesh Kumar's fate now rests not on guilt or innocence, but on a fundamental legal principle: "Bail is the rule; jail is the exception." Whether he walks out of court tomorrow—or remains behind bars while the truth is uncovered—depends on how well his lawyer can translate his human story into legal grounds for liberty. But one thing is certain: In a system where a three-second gesture can look like a crime... Presumption of innocence isn't just a right—it's a lifeline. Factual Anchors: • Accused: Rajesh Kumar, 28, sales executive, 4 years at Vijay Electronics • Offense: IPC 379 (Theft) of ₹45,000 laptop • Denies guilt; claims CCTV misinterpreted • Witness: Colleague Prakash • No criminal history • Family: Wife, child, father (shop owner), brother-in-law (teacher) • Strong community ties, stable residence in Bangalore • Offers passport surrender + two sureties • Laptop not recovered, investigation ongoing • Non-bailable offense (value > ₹5,000) • No co-accused, no recovered property, no prior cases`, practiceArea: 'Criminal Law', difficulty: 'Intermediate', estimatedTime: 30, rating: 4.7, reviewCount: 32, price: 0, // Free scenario tags: ['bail application', 'client interviewing', 'legal research', 'oral advocacy', 'investigation', 'evidence analysis', 'mystery'], status: 'published', createdAt: '2025-01-15T10:30:00Z', updatedAt: '2025-01-20T14:45:00Z', creator: { username: 'LegalExpert2025', name: 'Legal Training Institute' }, learningObjectives: [ 'Conduct a structured client interview to gather critical facts', 'Research applicable legal provisions under IPC and CrPC', 'Draft and orally present a bail application', 'Respond to standard prosecution objections', 'Apply legal principles to real-world scenarios', 'Develop critical thinking and advocacy skills' ], courtType: 'Magistrate Court', parties: [ { role: 'Defendant', name: 'Rajesh Kumar', description: 'Sales executive accused of theft. Claims innocence and proper procedure.' }, { role: 'Defense Attorney', name: 'You', description: 'Assigned to represent the defendant and uncover the truth.' }, { role: 'Prosecutor', name: 'State Prosecutor', description: 'Representing the state with circumstantial evidence against Rajesh.' }, { role: 'Key Witness', name: 'Prakash Mehta', description: 'Colleague who was present during the inventory check.' }, { role: 'Complainant', name: 'Mr. Desai', description: 'Store manager who reported the incident.' }, { role: 'Surety', name: 'Ramesh Kumar', description: "Rajesh's father who offers to be a surety." }, { role: 'Surety', name: 'Arjun Rao', description: "Rajesh's brother-in-law who offers to be a surety." } ], skillFocus: [ 'Client Interviewing', 'Legal Research', 'Oral Advocacy', 'Case Preparation', 'Critical Thinking', 'Evidence Analysis' ], duration: '20-30 minutes', assessmentCriteria: [ 'Thoroughness of client interview', 'Quality of legal research', 'Identification of evidence gaps', 'Strength of arguments presented', 'Oral advocacy skills', 'Professional courtroom demeanor', 'Understanding of bail principles' ], investigationPoints: [ 'CCTV footage analysis and interpretation', 'Digital inventory log examination', 'Witness credibility assessment', 'Employee background checks', 'Financial records of suspects', 'Timeline reconstruction' ], legalIssues: [ 'Bail application procedures under CrPC', 'Burden of proof in criminal cases', 'Circumstantial evidence evaluation', 'Right to legal representation', 'Presumption of innocence', 'Non-bailable offenses' ], evidenceToExamine: [ 'CCTV footage from multiple cameras', 'Digital inventory logs', 'Employee access records', 'Financial transaction records', 'Witness statements and alibis', 'Service center records' ], suspiciousActivities: [ 'Misinterpretation of CCTV footage', 'Falsified digital inventory log', 'Missing laptop with no recovery', 'Single witness account', 'No investigation of other employees', 'High-value item disappearance' ] }; const getDifficultyIcon = (difficulty: string) => { switch (difficulty) { case 'Beginner': return '🌱'; case 'Intermediate': return '📊'; case 'Advanced': return '🔥'; case 'Expert': return '👑'; default: return '📚'; } }; return (
